name.text: Calculating midpoint and plotting a frequency polygon from grouped data.

questiontext.text:
The following grouped data represent the distribution of exam scores for grade 10 students at Mpakani school.

Class Interval Frequency
0–10{@f1@}
10–20{@f2@}
20–30{@f3@}
30–40{@f4@}
40–50{@f5@}

1. Compute the class midpoints.

Enter them as a list:

[[input:ans_mid]] [[validation:ans_mid]]

2. Determine the coordinates of the points used to draw the frequency polygon.

Enter the coordinates as ordered pairs.



[[input:ans_poly]] [[validation:ans_poly]]

3. Plot the frequency polygon on the graph.

[[jsxgraph]] var board = JXG.JSXGraph.initBoard(divid,{ boundingbox:[-5,12,55,-1], axis:true, showCopyright:false }); /* Histogram bars */ board.create('polygon',[[0,0],[0,4],[10,4],[10,0]]); board.create('polygon',[[10,0],[10,7],[20,7],[20,0]]); board.create('polygon',[[20,0],[20,10],[30,10],[30,0]]); board.create('polygon',[[30,0],[30,6],[40,6],[40,0]]); board.create('polygon',[[40,0],[40,3],[50,3],[50,0]]); /* Points students should place */ var p1 = board.create('point',[5,4],{name:'',size:3}); var p2 = board.create('point',[15,7],{name:'',size:3}); var p3 = board.create('point',[25,10],{name:'',size:3}); var p4 = board.create('point',[35,6],{name:'',size:3}); var p5 = board.create('point',[45,3],{name:'',size:3}); /* Polygon line */ board.create('line',[p1,p2]); board.create('line',[p2,p3]); board.create('line',[p3,p4]); board.create('line',[p4,p5]); [[/jsxgraph]]

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Step 1: Determine the class midpoints

The midpoint of a class interval is found using the formula

\[ \text{Midpoint}=\frac{\text{Lower boundary}+\text{Upper boundary}}{2}. \]

Applying this to each class interval:

  • \((0+10)/2 = 5\)
  • \((10+20)/2 = 15\)
  • \((20+30)/2 = 25\)
  • \((30+40)/2 = 35\)
  • \((40+50)/2 = 45\)

Therefore, the list of class midpoints is

\[ {@ta_mid@} \] ---

Step 2: Determine the frequency polygon points

A frequency polygon is obtained by plotting points whose coordinates are:

\[ (\text{class midpoint},\ \text{frequency}) \]

Using the midpoints and the frequencies from the table we obtain:

  • \(({@m1@}, {@f1@})\)
  • \(({@m2@}, {@f2@})\)
  • \(({@m3@}, {@f3@})\)
  • \(({@m4@}, {@f4@})\)
  • \(({@m5@}, {@f5@})\)

So the frequency polygon points are

\[ ({@m1@},{@f1@}),({@m2@},{@f2@}),({@m3@},{@f3@}),({@m4@},{@f4@}),({@m5@},{@f5@}) \] ---

Step 3: Construct the frequency polygon

Plot the points above on the graph and connect them with straight line segments in order of increasing midpoint.

The resulting polygon shows how the frequencies vary across the class intervals and provides a visual representation of the distribution.

Frequency polygons are useful because they make it easier to compare distributions and identify trends in the data.

questiondescription.text:
Monica SHERILY

Given a frequency table for the distribution scores of an exam.The student is required to draw a histogram to show the distribution of the exams marks.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done.

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.
